stimulus: Histories of the Asian American movement—a coalition that pressed for political and cultural recognition and that crested in the late 1960s and 1970s—tend to spotlight the most confrontational student organizers in major coastal cities, leaving the impression of a movement uniformly radical. Sociologist Mai-Linh Tran has argued, however, that if attention is shifted to less-publicized initiatives in suburban community centers, mutual-aid clinics, and language schools, one finds participants espousing a wide span of political stances—reformist, religious, and liberal as well as radical—within the same broad cause.
stem: Which choice best describes the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole?

answerOptions.content: It introduces a trend in scholarship on the Asian American movement that the text claims has now been completely abandoned in light of Tran's research.
It identifies a focus in scholarship on the Asian American movement that, according to the text, conceals the political variety actually present among participants.
It calls out an emphasis that the text implies has been driven by historians' own ideological commitments.
It summarizes a conventional approach that, the text suggests, misrepresents the effectiveness of community institutions.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The underlined clause names the customary scholarly focus on confrontational coastal student organizers; the rest of the passage uses Tran's work to argue that this focus hides the wider political diversity revealed in lower-profile, community-based settings.

Choice A is incorrect because the text only counterposes Tran's argument; it does not claim the prior trend has been abandoned. Choice C is incorrect because the text never attributes the focus to historians' political commitments. Choice D is incorrect because the contrast concerns ideological range, not the effectiveness of community institutions.
